title: Rigid heddle scarf with leno lace inserts
subtitle: A plain-weave rigid heddle scarf with open leno-lace panels created with a pick-up stick
excerpt: Leno lace on the rigid heddle is made by using a pick-up stick to cross pairs of warp threads, locking them in that twisted position for several picks before releasing. The twisted warp threads grip the weft and create an open, lacy structure within the plain-weave ground.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from rigid heddle pick-up stick technique references and public-domain leno lace weaving documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads stretched lengthwise on the loom under tension, through which the weft is interlaced.
The thread carried horizontally across the warp on each pick (row) of weaving.
The number of warp threads per unit of width, stated in ends per inch (EPI) or ends per centimetre.
The most basic weave structure: the weft passes over one warp thread and under the next, alternating on each pick.
An open weave structure in which pairs of warp threads are crossed (twisted) around each other and held in that crossed position by the weft, creating a stable open mesh.
A flat, smooth stick used to manually select individual warp threads and hold them in a raised or lowered position to create a pattern shed different from the standard heddle shed.
On a rigid heddle loom, the slotted and holed plate that holds the warp threads and creates the shed when lifted or lowered.
The V-shaped opening between raised and lowered warp threads through which the shuttle passes.
A single pass of the weft through the shed.
The woven edge of the cloth on each side, formed by the weft looping around the outermost warp threads.
Washing and pressing the woven cloth off the loom to set the fibres, even out the weave, and achieve the intended hand.

content.text: Before the draft, here is a plain-English description of how
works on the rigid heddle. In standard
threads remain parallel and upright. In leno, the weaver uses a
to reach past each slot thread and lift the hole thread on the right of it, crossing the two threads over each other. This crossed position is held by the pick-up stick while several picks of
. When the pick-up stick is removed and the
returns to its normal position, the crossed warp threads relax slightly but the weft locks them in the twisted position, creating stable open lace holes.
This scarf finishes at approximately 180 × 28 cm. The

is 10 EPI (4 ends/cm) for an open, drapey weave. Use a medium-weight singles or plied yarn in cotton, linen, or a cotton-linen blend: leno lace works especially clearly in non-elastic yarns that hold the twist. Warp to approximately 230 cm (including loom waste) at a width of 30 cm in the heddle. Total warp ends: 30 cm × 4 ends/cm = 120 ends.

items.name: Medium-weight cotton or cotton-linen yarn, warp and weft
Rigid heddle loom with at least 30 cm weaving width
10-dent rigid heddle (1 thread per slot or hole = 10 EPI)
Flat pick-up stick, at least 30 cm long

content.text: Reading the draft: shaft 1 represents hole threads and shaft 2 represents slot threads in the rigid heddle. Plain weave alternates the two shafts. The leno sections are not shown in a standard threading draft: they are a manual pick-up operation that modifies the hole-slot arrangement during weaving.

Wind 120 ends on the warping board to 230 cm. Thread alternate ends through holes and slots across the 30 cm width of the heddle. Tie onto the front apron rod in small, even bouts. Weave a 3 cm plain-weave header to spread the warp.

The scarf alternates sections of plain weave with sections of leno lace. A suggested sequence: 5 cm of plain weave, then 1 cm of leno lace, repeating for the full length. Adjust the ratio based on the look wanted: more plain weave gives a more solid cloth; more leno gives a more open, lacy cloth.
For plain-weave sections: lift the heddle up (hole threads rise), pass the shuttle through, lower the heddle down (slot threads rise), pass the shuttle back through. Beat each
to approximately 4 picks per cm. Keep the

For leno lace sections: lower the heddle to the down position (slot threads rise). The pick-up stick goes in at this point. Working from right to left, use the pick-up stick to reach past each raised slot thread and bring up the hole thread to its right, placing it on the pick-up stick. Continue across the full warp width, picking up alternating pairs. When the pick-up stick has been loaded with all the hole threads, turn it on its edge to hold the threads up and create the leno shed. Pass the shuttle through from right to left. Weave 3 to 5 picks in this leno shed, keeping the pick-up stick in place. Then remove the pick-up stick, return the heddle to neutral, and resume plain weave.
After weaving the leno section, work 2 picks of plain weave before the next leno section. These ground picks stabilise the leno holes and prevent the lace rows from bunching.
Weave 3 cm of plain weave at each end and knot the warp ends in groups of 4, trimming fringe to 4 cm. Wet finish by hand-washing in cool water (30 °C) and pressing gently with a damp cloth. The
consolidates the lace holes and the plain-weave ground simultaneously. Lay flat to dry and press while slightly damp.

items.problem: Lace holes close up after wet finishing
items.solution: The weft beat in the leno sections was too heavy, packing the picks together too tightly. In the leno sections, beat very lightly: the open structure only works if the picks are spaced apart.

items.problem: Pick-up stick is hard to slide across the warp
items.solution: The warp tension is too high. Ease the back beam slightly and try again. The pick-up stick should glide across without catching.

items.problem: Leno sections are at different spacings across the width
items.solution: The pick-up sequence was skipped on some pairs. Reload the pick-up stick carefully, checking that every hole thread has been picked up before turning the stick on its edge.

items.problem: Selvedge loops at the leno sections
items.solution: The weft is entering the leno shed at too wide an arc. Reduce the arc in the leno shed: the open structure needs less take-up allowance than plain weave.
